food_name: Yangjing Bajing Dessert Platter (燕京八景)
food_subgroup: Traditional Chinese confectionery and sweet pastry assortment

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a Chinese imperial dessert platter. FOODON_03315091 (confectionery food product) is the closest defensible parent category covering sweet confectionery items including glutinous rice-based sweets, bean-paste pastries, and nut-filled confections.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Confectionery and related products) is the closest defensible parent for this mixed traditional Chinese dessert platter. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for this composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific traditional Chinese dessert platter. SNOMED CT coverage for regional confectionery composites is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared dessert platter typically weighing approximately 400 g, intended for 2–4 diners, containing an assortment of 6–8 individual traditional Chinese imperial dessert pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Food Composition Table (CFCT) 6th Edition — Glutinous rice-based confectionery and bean-paste pastry composite; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for glutinous rice flour, red bean paste, soybean flour, and mixed nuts
nutrient_source.organization: Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention (China CDC) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition modelling using CFCT 6th Edition values for lǘ dǎ gǔn (rolling donkey, glutinous rice roll with red bean paste and soybean flour) and analogous Chinese glutinous rice confectionery items, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (NDB 20052 glutinous rice, NDB 16014 red beans cooked, NDB 12195 sesame seeds, NDB 12155 walnuts). The platter is described as containing wheat, beans, and nuts. Estimated macros reflect a blended profile of glutinous rice flour dough (~50%), sweetened bean paste filling (~30%), and nut/sesame coating (~20%). Values are per 100 g of platter as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice and bean-paste confectionery — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283; glutinous rice GI ~98, red bean paste GI ~29, blended weighted estimate for composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific platter. GI estimated as weighted blend: glutinous rice dough component (GI ~98, University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice) weighted at 50%, sweetened red bean paste component (GI ~29, University of Sydney GI Database) weighted at 30%, and nut/sesame coating (GI ~15 estimated for mixed nuts, University of Sydney GI Database) weighted at 20%. Blended GI = (0.50×98)+(0.30×29)+(0.20×15) = 49+8.7+3 = ~61, rounded to 65 to account for added sugar content. GL per serving = (65 × 51.5 × 400/100) / 100 = 33.5 g per 400 g platter serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~55 mg per 100 g); no added salt in traditional preparation
hypertension.guidance: This dessert platter is low in sodium (~220 mg per 400 g serving) and poses minimal direct hypertensive risk; however, portion control is advised given the moderate caloric density, and individuals on antihypertensive medications should monitor total carbohydrate intake to avoid glycemic-mediated blood pressure fluctuations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (51.5 g per 100 g) dominated by glutinous rice flour with a medium-to-high estimated glycemic index (~65); significant sugar load (~18 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this platter due to its high refined carbohydrate and sugar content; a single shared serving (400 g) delivers approximately 206 g of carbohydrate with an estimated glycemic load of 33.5, which substantially exceeds recommended single-meal carbohydrate targets and warrants strict portion limitation or avoidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (6.2 g per 100 g) with low saturated fat (0.9 g); presence of beneficial unsaturated fats from walnuts and sesame; high sugar content is a secondary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of this platter is relatively favourable for cardiovascular health due to the predominance of unsaturated fats from nuts and sesame; however, the high sugar content (~72 g per 400 g serving) may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia and should be limited in individuals with established cardiovascular disease or metabolic syndrome.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Yangjing Bajing Dessert Platter (燕京八景甜品拼盘) is a curated assortment of traditional Beijing imperial court confections served as a shared dessert experience. The name '燕京八景' references the Eight Scenic Spots of Yanjing (ancient Beijing), evoking the cultural heritage of the Qing and Ming dynasty royal kitchens. The platter typically includes lǘ dǎ gǔn (驴打滚, Rolling Donkey) — glutinous rice rolls coated in roasted soybean flour and filled with sweetened red bean paste — alongside other handcrafted items such as sesame-crusted glutinous rice cakes, walnut pastries, jujube-filled dumplings, and peanut brittle-style confections. All items are made without preservatives and are lightly sweetened. The texture profile spans chewy glutinous rice dough, smooth bean paste, crunchy nut coatings, and soft steamed pastry. Nutritionally, the platter is carbohydrate-dominant (~51.5 g per 100 g), with moderate protein from beans and nuts (~5.8 g per 100 g) and a modest fat contribution from sesame and walnuts (~6.2 g per 100 g). The estimated glycemic index is medium (~65) due to the blended effect of high-GI glutinous rice and low-GI bean paste and nuts. Clinically, this platter is high-concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its substantial carbohydrate and sugar load, moderate-concern for cardiovascular disease due to sugar content, and low-concern for hypertension given its minimal sodium. It contains gluten (wheat), tree nuts, peanuts, sesame, and soy, making allergen disclosure essential. This dish is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) restaurant in Singapore and is priced at SGD 49.40 per platter, reflecting its artisanal, preservative-free preparation.

rolling donkey: glutinous rice roll with bean paste

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition modelling using Chinese Food Composition Table (CFCT) 6th Edition data for analogous glutinous rice-based confectionery (lǘ dǎ gǔn and related items) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for component ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for glutinous rice and red bean paste. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component ingredients are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from CFCT 6th Edition and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy using weighted-average composition modelling; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact platter. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): traditional Chinese confectionery of this type does not use hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per CFCT analogous entries. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): all components are plant-based (glutinous rice, beans, nuts, sesame); no animal-derived ingredients reported in the dish description. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the CFCT analogous entries for glutinous rice confectionery and bean-paste items. 5. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the CFCT analogous entries used for this estimation. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this is a plant-based confectionery with no animal-derived ingredients; B12 is not present and not reported in source. 7. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the CFCT analogous entries for this food category. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the CFCT analogous entries; plant-based confectionery does not naturally contain vitamin D. 9. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the CFCT analogous entries used. 10. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the CFCT analogous entries for this food category. 11. GI is an analogous estimate (see gi_source); no direct GI measurement exists for this platter. 12. The input description contains a likely typographical error: 'Whoat (guten)' is interpreted as 'Wheat (gluten)'. 13. Serving size of 400 g is estimated for a shared platter for 2–4 diners based on typical restaurant portion conventions; actual weight may vary. 14. Halal status is unconfirmed; the restaurant has not been verified as halal-certified. 15. Vegan status is uncertain; some traditional Chinese confectionery items may use lard or egg; marked false pending confirmation.
